text: The surgical view reveals a large myoma occupying the entire abdominal cavity.
The surgeon visually identifies a large myoma occupying the entire abdominal cavity of the patient.
The surgeon presents the largest myoma ever removed, visibly occupying the entire abdominal cavity.
The surgeon displays the morselated myoma on a digital weighing machine, showing its total weight after removal from the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon displays the entire myoma that has been morselated, showing its size and appearance after removal.
The camera zooms in on the digital scale displaying the weight of the morselated myoma tissue.
The video shows a collection of fluid, likely central degenerated fluid from the myoma, being displayed.
The video shows the central degenerated fluid being expelled from the surgical site.
The patient's abdomen appears flat and without distension as preparations are made for extubation.
The surgeon inserts the varus needle at the Palmer's point on the patient's abdomen, preparing for laparoscopic access.

The surgeon performs an irrigation test, followed by a suction test and hanging drop test, before introducing the varus needle through the palmar's point, located two centimeters above the costal margin, known as Mishra's point. The abdomen is then insufflated.
The surgeon adjusts the insufflation pressure to eighteen millimeters of mercury to create more space within the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon enlarges the incision to accommodate an 11 mm optical trocar, following the completion of pneumoperitoneum. This step is crucial to prevent compression of the vena cava by the large myoma, which could affect venous return.

The surgeon introduces the first trocar at Palmer's point, with the abdomen fully insufflated. The telescope is then inserted through this trocar, navigating towards the paracolic gutter, where it encounters the large fibroid.

The surgeon navigates laterally through the paracolic gutter, applying the baseball diamond concept for port placement. The subsidence myoma is visible, characterized by its pedicle. The second port is positioned along the anterior axillary line.

The surgeon introduces a second port approximately ten centimeters away from the initial port under direct vision. A third port is also placed along the same line, located in the epigastric region, following the baseball diamond concept.

The surgeon positions the telescope approximately three centimeters below and left of the xiphoid process, initially placing it in the epigastric region. The telescope will later be repositioned to accommodate a 10 mm trocar for optimal visualization.
All surgical ports are positioned on the abdomen, ready for the procedure to commence.

The surgeon uses a grasper in each hand to manipulate the patient's position, tilting the head down and to the right. The telescope view reveals a large myoma occupying the abdominal cavity, extending up to the costal margin.

The telescope is positioned at the GP sternum, providing a view of the fibroid extending above the costal margin. The fibroid appears pedunculated and is located near the paracolic gutter, where the surgeon is navigating downward. The fibroid shows signs of torsion, which is likely causing severe pain for the patient.
The surgeon observes the large torted myoma pressing against the cecum and small intestine, causing visible distortion in the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon attempts to induce torsion on the pedunculated myoma, which is visible occupying a significant portion of the abdominal cavity.
The round ligament on the right side is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the ovary and fallopian tube, noting the presence of torsion. An attempt is made to detort the ovary, but complete detorsion is challenging due to the presence of a large myoma. The fallopian tube on the opposite side is also visible.
The surgeon manipulates the moving myoma and applies an extracorporeal Misra's knot to secure it.
The surgeon positions the telescope to view the lower part of the surgical field, focusing on the pedicle at the base of the myoma.

The surgeon changes the telescope position to the middle to visualize the pedicle from below. A number one Y-curl suture is used to encircle the pedicle, and it is then brought out from the opposite side.

The surgeon manipulates the left side structures towards the right, revealing the infundibulopelvic ligament and round ligament being pushed towards the upper abdomen. The suture is fed through, and an extracorporeal Misra's knot is tied, capable of securing pedicles up to twenty-two millimeters.
The surgeon removes the suture from the surgical field, preparing to tie the extracorporeal Misra's knot outside the body.
The assistant uses a myoma screw to elevate the myoma, enhancing the definition of the pedicle at its base.
The surgeon tightens the Misra knot, configured in a one-one-one-one-one pattern, around the pedicle, ensuring secure ligation.

The surgeon demonstrates the knot-tying technique, performing one hitch, one wind, and one lock, followed by a second wind and lock, and a third wind and lock. The knot is then tightened over the pedicle.
The surgeon ties a knot without using vasopressin, typically used for intramural myoma, and begins ligature approximately one centimeter above the knot.
The surgeon uses both a ligature and harmonic scalpel to enucleate and detach the targeted tissue from its surrounding structures.
The telescope is positioned in the lumbar area to provide a clear view, as the initial palmar point entry resulted in obstruction by the myoma, necessitating a change in strategy.
The surgeon uses a ligature device to coagulate and separate tissue, proceeding cautiously to minimize bleeding during the procedure.
The surgeon uses the harmonic scalpel to continue the enucleation process, detaching tissue with precision.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect tissue, demonstrating the separation process with a new harmonic probe model.
The surgeon encounters slight bleeding and oozing while dissecting a highly vascular myoma with large blood vessels, proceeding cautiously to separate the tissue.
The surgeon uses a combination of ligature and harmonic devices, with the ligature functioning as a vessel sealer and the harmonic serving as a dissector, to manage tissue separation and hemostasis.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel with its vibrating jaw positioned upwards to carefully lift and cut the pedicle of the myoma, ensuring slow and controlled separation to minimize bleeding.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to slowly separate the myoma from surrounding tissue, carefully dissecting to avoid bleeding from the large blood vessels.

The surgeon carefully cuts through the tissue, revealing large blood vessels that are being coagulated. An extracorporeal knot is applied to prevent bleeding, effectively complementing the use of energy devices.
The surgeon rearranges the myoma screw from the opposite side to pull the large myoma over the surface of the liver, providing better access for further manipulation.
The surgeon uses a myoma screw inserted through the epigastric port to pull the large myoma over the anterior surface of the liver, facilitating continued myomectomy.

Two myoma screws are used, one from the epigastric port and another from the Palmer's port, to pull the myoma over the anterior surface of the liver. The pedicle, including part of the uterus, is gradually cut while the knot secures the area to prevent bleeding.
The surgeon continues to cut the pedicle, with approximately seventy-five percent already severed, while the remaining portion is being carefully dissected.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers above the right ovary, ensuring its protection while continuing to cut the remaining pedicle of the myoma.
The surgeon uses a ligature device to perform desiccation on the cut edge of tissue, addressing visible oozing to control bleeding.
The surgeon achieves hemostasis to stop bleeding before detaching the myoma, ensuring that the pedicle remains accessible and preventing increased bleeding once tension is released.

The surgeon carefully approaches the final detachment of the myoma, ensuring hemostasis to prevent increased bleeding. The ovary is visible and protected during the procedure, with considerations for preserving or sacrificing the ovary based on the patient's age.
An instrument is used to elevate the entire pedicle, ensuring visibility and preventing accidental damage to underlying structures such as the bowel.
The surgeon tightens a knot, creating a dumbbell-shaped structure, and proceeds to cut the last part of the tissue.

The surgeon plans to reinforce the initial knot by tying an additional pre-tied loop, known as Mistra's knot, around the large pedicle of the myoma, which has been entirely detached from the uterus. This step aims to augment the dumbbell shape of the first knot and reduce the risk of re-bleeding.
The surgeon applies a second knot to complement the first knot on the pedicle, ensuring hemostasis and preventing any bleeding. The knot is tightened securely at the base of the pedicle.
The surgeon tightens the knot securely around the pedicle, augmenting the initial knot to prevent bleeding.
The surgeon tightens the knot around the pedicle, preparing to cut it. The pedicle is pulled towards the gallbladder, indicating the next step of morselation.

The surgeon identifies a para-ovarian cyst on the left side of the ovary. The cyst is visually apparent and requires removal. The surgeon approaches the infundibulopelvic ligament to excise the cyst without additional dissection.
The surgeon removes the fallopian tube from the same side as the previously excised ovarian cyst, ensuring complete removal of the tube as part of the procedure.
The surgeon removes a para ovarian cyst, cutting the infundibulopelvic ligament. The procedure progresses towards the major salpings.
The surgeon identifies the major salpings and removes the tube, leaving six millimeters of the tube near the uterus.

The surgeon completes the ovarian cystectomy and prepares to remove the cyst using a morcellator, ensuring no small tissue remains inside the abdomen before proceeding with the cholecystectomy and appendectomy.
The surgeon uses a morselator to fragment and remove the tube and para ovarian cyst from the surgical field.
The surgeon begins morselating the myoma, using a morselator to fragment the fibroid tissue for removal.

The myoma is positioned in the epigastric region and is being pulled upwards. The telescope is located in the Palmer's point, while the myoma screw is in the lumbar area to facilitate morselation. The myoma is large, requiring continuous adjustment of the telescope to view different sections as morselation progresses.

The laparoscopic view shows the ongoing morselation of the large myoma, with the morselator gradually reducing the size of the tissue. The process is lengthy, taking significant time due to the myoma's size.

The video intermittently shows the process of morselation, where the large myoma is being gradually fragmented and extracted. The morselator device is actively engaged in cutting and removing tissue, although the entire process is not continuously visible due to the size of the myoma.

The surgeon continues the process of morselating the myoma, ensuring that the tissue is drawn towards the morselator rather than moving the morselator towards the tissue. The video shows a portion of the one-hour morselation process, which is part of a total two-hour procedure.

The surgeon performs a cholecystectomy with the telescope positioned in the lumbar area and an instrument in the palmar's point, deviating from the typical port placement. The gallbladder is being manipulated despite the challenging ergonomics due to the unconventional angles of manipulation, azimuth, and elevation.
The surgeon demonstrates the port placement with the telescope positioned in the lumbar area. An anteromedial traction is applied to create a posterior peritoneal window.
The surgeon is positioned above the Rouviere's sulcus, creating the posterior peritoneum by applying anteromedial traction, gradually exposing the peritoneal layer.

The surgeon performs dissection from the left side to create the posterior peritoneal window, ensuring the critical view of safety. Two structures are clearly visible leading towards the gallbladder, and the inferior surface of the liver is exposed.
The surgeon identifies the artery and vein, creating a window between them. The left lobe of the liver is visible, obstructing the view due to the incorrect positioning of the telescope.
The surgeon skillfully separates the posterior peritoneum, followed by stripping the anterior peritoneum. A window is then created between the artery and the duct.

The interior window between the artery and duct is visible, along with the window between the duct and the inferior surface of the liver. The surgeon performs final stripping in this area to complete the dissection.

The surgeon performs mechanical stripping to enlarge the window between the cystic duct and artery, with the inferior surface of the liver clearly visible. The common bile duct (CBD) is identified, and the procedure is conducted above the Rouvière's sulcus.

The surgeon uses a clip applicator at Palmer's point to further enlarge the posterior window at the porta hepatis, near the level of the Rouvier's sulcus, ensuring the inferior surface of the liver remains visible.
The surgeon achieves a critical view of safety, focusing on posterior peritoneal dissection to separate the gallbladder from the liver, with clear visibility of anatomical structures.
The surgeon enlarges the anterior and posterior windows, further skeletonizing the structures between the artery and duct, revealing clear anatomical planes.
The surgeon approaches from the anterior side to enlarge the anterior window, ensuring clear visibility of the artery and duct without any tenting of the common bile duct (CBD).
The surgeon positions the duct below the artery, both structures clearly visible in the surgical field. A titanium clip is prepared for application to secure the duct.
The surgeon applies a titanium clip over the cystic duct, avoiding lateral clipping. The common hepatic duct is visible in the foreground, with the cystic duct appearing relatively short.

The surgeon applies a single titanium clip to the artery, preparing to use a harmonic scalpel for coagulation. The cystic duct, which is of average size and slightly dilated, is visible, and lateral clipping is avoided.
The surgeon uses a Soundreach probe to cut the cystic duct, following the application of a titanium clip.

The surgeon carefully separates the posterior peritoneum while applying anteromedial traction to the gallbladder. The right shoulder of the gallbladder is cut using anterolateral traction to prevent rupture, facilitating its separation from the liver.
The gallbladder is being carefully extracted from the liver bed, with the surgeon ensuring a slow and controlled removal to maintain patient safety.
The surgeon completes the removal of the gallbladder, concluding the cholecystectomy procedure. Blood is visible in the upper abdomen, attributed to the myoma, and is being suctioned out.
The surgeon inspects the myoma pedicle, confirming its integrity and noting that there are no visible issues with the structure.
The surgeon grasps the appendix and lifts it to perform a laparoscopic appendectomy using a ligature. The gallbladder is also held in place during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a ligature harmonic device to coagulate and cut the mesoappendix, pulling it towards the cecum during the appendectomy procedure.

The surgeon mobilizes the ileum from the paracolic gutter, performing lateral peritoneal mobilization. The major appendix is cut at its base, near the visible tinea coli, ensuring complete removal of the appendix.
The surgeon reaches the base of the appendix during the laparoscopic procedure, ensuring the dissection extends to the tinea coli to avoid leaving an incomplete appendix.

The surgeon uses a pre-tied Misra knot, threading it through a loop and tying it at the base of the appendix. The knot pusher is positioned over the cecum, causing the knot to shrink securely at the base.

The appendix is removed through the 15 mm morcellator port, followed by the extraction of the gallbladder through the same port. The surgical field is then inspected, focusing on the cul-de-sac for any collected blood, as the myoma pedicle is present and no drain will be used.

The surgeon lifts the bowel to reveal collected blood in the surgical field. The blood is not fresh, indicating it accumulated during the myomectomy procedure. The suction device is used to remove the pooled blood.

The surgeon performs final suction of collected blood in the cul-de-sac, inspecting the visible sacral promontory and right ureter. The ureter appears relieved from previous pressure due to myoma removal. The pararectal area is inspected for fragmented myoma tissue, with slight bleeding observed. The rectum is gently pulled up for better visualization.
The surgeon closes the 15mm port site, ensuring proper closure of the incision. The Palmer's port, located above the costal margin over the bone, remains open and does not require closure.

The surgeon uses a cobbler's needle to close the 15 mm port, ensuring all layers except the skin are included to prevent incisional hernia. The final closure is completed, and the abdomen appears lax after the removal of a large fibroid.
